title: Rent Control: The Ceiling Trap - Marginal REVOLUTION
gist: Alex Tabarrok highlights economist Are Oust's analysis of four decades of Oslo newspaper housing classifieds (1970-2008), showing that under rent control tenants had to compete for landlords via employer vouching, huge deposits, and non-price discrimination, and that these distortions vanished almost immediately once Norway abolished rent control in 1982.

insights.claim: Economist Are Oust documented Norway's 1982 rent-control abolition by collecting Oslo newspaper Aftenposten's housing classifieds from 1970 to 2008, creating a natural experiment on how a rental market behaves before and after price controls.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: A rare, long-run natural experiment on rent control's real-world effects, useful as reference evidence whenever rent-control policy proposals resurface in U.S. cities.
insights.quote: He collected housing classifieds from Oslo’s Aftenposten from 1970 to 2008 and watched the market turn inside out.
insights.locator: Norway abolished its rent control in 1982

insights.claim: Under Oslo's rent control, tenants had to advertise themselves to landlords rather than the reverse — 'housing wanted' ads outnumbered 'housing for rent' ads, inverting the normal buyer/seller advertising pattern of a housing market.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: Direct empirical evidence that price ceilings flip housing into a seller's-favor rationing market, a pattern to expect in any jurisdiction imposing binding rent caps.

insights.claim: About 10 to 15 percent of tenant-placed housing ads in rent-controlled Oslo included a vouching statement from the tenant's employer, functioning like a bank guarantee to substitute for price-based screening of tenants.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: Shows how non-price rationing generates informal credentialing markets (employer vouching) as a side effect of suppressed prices.
insights.quote: Ten to fifteen percent of those ads were placed by the tenant’s employer, vouching for them the way a bank vouches for a borrower.
insights.locator: Under rent control, Oslo’s listings pages

insights.claim: With prices capped, Oslo landlords screened tenants on non-price traits instead — some ads explicitly demanded a tenant of a particular gender, age, occupation, or region of origin, with some specifying 'strong Christian beliefs.'
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: Documents a concrete mechanism by which price controls enable discrimination that a functioning price mechanism would otherwise crowd out — a durable talking point against rent-control proposals.
insights.locator: Landlords, for their part, could demand

insights.claim: Under rent control, Oslo security deposits commonly ran 50 to 60 months of rent and occasionally 100 or more, effectively making tenants interest-free lenders of the landlord's home equity.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: Quantifies how far non-price rationing can distort capital allocation — deposits functioning as disguised interest-free landlord financing — a mechanism relevant to modeling rent-control side effects elsewhere.
insights.quote: Deposits commonly ran to 50 or 60 months’ rent, occasionally 100 or more: tenants effectively lent the landlord the equity of the flat, interest free.
insights.locator: Deposits commonly ran to 50 or 60 months

themes.theme: What happens when the ceiling lifts

insights.claim: Under Oslo's rent control only about 20 percent of 'for rent' ads printed the rent (since the true price was often illegal), but within a few years of the 1982 abolition roughly 80 percent of listings printed an asking rent.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: A stark before/after data point on price transparency that can be cited directly in any rent-control policy debate.
insights.quote: only about 20 percent of “for rent” ads dared print the rent, much of which would have been illegal.

insights.claim: Once Norway lifted rent control, landlords resumed advertising to tenants (rather than vice versa), mega-deposits vanished, and demands for gender-, age-, or religion-specific tenants disappeared, as price resumed doing the market's rationing.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: Demonstrates that suppressing price signals is what generates non-price rationing distortions, and that removing the cap restores normal market function quickly — the core thesis for opposing rent control.
insights.quote: The price went back to doing the rationing — so nothing else had to.
